topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy paper felt slick beneath my fingertips as I unfolded the document. It was my life story. My accomplishments, my struggles, the entire narrative of who I was. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly as I scanned the first paragraph. "Elias Vance, a titan of industry, whose ascent was marked by ruthless ambition..." I blinked, rereading the sentence. *Ruthless ambition?* I hadn't ever described myself that way. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in my chest, making my palms sweat. I dropped the paper onto the mahogany desk with a sharp *thwack*. "This is absurd," I muttered, the sound rough in my throat. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the study, my movements jerky and agitated. Every word seemed deliberately misaligned with the reality I inhabited. I needed answers, and this paper, this carefully constructed falsehood, was the source.

[story 2]

I stared at the biography projected onto the screen, the words swimming before my eyes. Every accolade, every pivotal moment, was there, yet the thread connecting them to *me* felt severed. A tight knot formed just beneath my sternum, pulling at my shirt collar. I leaned closer to the monitor, my breath catching in shallow gasps. "No," I whispered, the sound barely audible over the hum of the ventilation system. I tapped the screen with a rigid finger, tracing a line of text that detailed a personal triumph I’d never experienced. My gaze flickered around the room, sharp and assessing, as if searching for the architect of this deception. I turned away from the screen, running a hand roughly through my hair, a gesture of pure frustration. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to steady the rapid thrumming beneath my ribs.

[story 3]

The silence in the archive room was oppressive, broken only by the rustle of old paper. I held the manuscript—my life story—with two hands, the edges crinkling under the pressure. My knuckles were white. When I reached the section detailing my early career, a profound sense of dislocation washed over me. It wasn't anger that surfaced first, but a deep, unsettling sense of wrongness. I let out a short, sharp exhale, a sound devoid of warmth. "This is not right," I stated, my voice clipped and low. I began to flip through the pages rapidly, my eyes darting from sentence to sentence, searching for the intentional error, the slip that betrayed the author's oversight. Each passage confirmed the fabrication, and a stiff tension settled in my shoulders, making it difficult to breathe deeply. I finally slammed the book shut, the resulting sound echoing loudly in the quiet space.
